m_Item1.QuestionText: What function does an operating system’s scheduling mechanism perform?

Options.OptionText: It manages how computing tasks are prioritized for CPU access.
It decides which peripherals to connect to the system.
It organizes the installation of software applications.
It determines how to label and format disk drives.

m_Item1.Explanation: Scheduling is the process by which an operating system decides which tasks or processes receive CPU time and in what order, thereby managing resources efficiently.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following schedules the threads
m_Item1.Explanation: OS schedules the threads. It process wants to create a thread, a call will be given to kernel. If the thread created is at a user levelcall dose not go to OS

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a process fails, most operating systems write the error information to a .....
m_Item1.Explanation: When a process fails, most operating systems writes an  error information to a log file. A log file is a file that records events that occur within an operating system or software application. The log file can contain information about system errors, security events, and other types of system activity.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The kernel is _______ of user threads.
m_Item1.Explanation: The kernel is typically unaware of user threads, which are managed by user-level thread libraries. User threads are created and managed within a user-level process, and the kernel is only aware of the process as a whole.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Since  the kernel thread management is done by the Operating System itself ......

Options.OptionText: kernel threads are faster to create than user threads
kernel threads are slower to create than user threads
kernel threads are easier to manage as well as create then user threads

m_Item1.Explanation: Since the kernel thread management is done by the Operating System itself, it can create and manage kernel threads more efficiently than user threads. Kernel threads are created and managed in kernel space, while user threads are created and managed in user space.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which one of the following is not shared by threads?
m_Item1.Explanation: Threads maintain their individual stack and registers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A thread is also called ___________
m_Item1.Explanation: A thread is called as light weight process since it sahres code and data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A system is in the safe state if ____________

Options.OptionText: The system can allocate resources to each process in some order and still avoid a deadlock .
There exist a safe sequence for resource allocation.
The degree of multiprogramming is high.

m_Item1.Explanation: If any resource is allocated to the process and the system dose not end in deadlock situation.....

m_Item1.QuestionText: The link between two processes P and Q to send and receive messages is called __________
m_Item1.Explanation: The link between two processes P and Q to send and receive messages is called communication link. Two processes P and Q want to communicate with each other; there should be a communication link that must exist between these two processes so that both processes can able to send and receive messages using that link.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Interprocess communication ......

Options.OptionText: allows processes to communicate and synchronize their actions using the same address space
allows processes to communicate and synchronize their actions
allows the processes to only synchronize their actions without communication

m_Item1.Explanation:
Interprocess Communication allows processes to communicate and synchronize their actions. Interprocess Communication (IPC) mechanism is used by cooperating processes to exchange data and information.
There are two models of IPC:
→ Shared Memory
→ Message Passing

m_Item1.QuestionText: Inter-process Communication requires ________?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inter process communication requires process synchronization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Concurrent access to shared data may result in ____________
m_Item1.Explanation:
Concurrent access to shared data results in data inconsistency.
Concurrent access to shared data can result in data inconsistency if multiple processes or threads modify the same data simultaneously without proper synchronization. This can lead to race conditions, where the outcome of the computation depends on the order in which the processes or threads access the shared data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The purpose of long term scheduler is

Options.OptionText: To bring processes into ready queue .
To move the processes into the swap area on local disk.

m_Item1.Explanation: Long term scheduler picks the process from job queue and delievers it to ready queue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Binary Semaphore has

Options.OptionText: Value returnes by lines in critical section.

m_Item1.Explanation: Binary semaphore is a synchronization tool which gererates binary values 0 or one depending on the accessibility of critical section.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens in context switching

Options.OptionText: CUP terminates one process and takes another process for execution.
There is switching between the programs.
CPU switches from one process to another by saving status of current process and loading status of next process.
Process deletes it's entry from Process Control Table.

m_Item1.Explanation: CPU switches from one process to another  by saving status of current process and loading status of next process. Timer decides the switching time for the process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The processes that enter the system are allocated  into a ___________________.
m_Item1.Explanation: Process entered the system is brought into the job queue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If the CPU is Executing any operating system instructions,the machine is said to be in ______mode
m_Item1.Explanation:
When the CPU is executing operating system instructions, the machine is said to be in system mode, also known as kernel mode or privileged mode. In this mode, the CPU has full access to all system resources and can execute privileged instructions that are not available to user-mode processes. This allows the operating system to manage the system's resources and provide services to applications.
In contrast, when the CPU is executing user-mode instructions, the machine is said to be in user mode, also known as application mode or unprivileged mode. In this mode, the CPU has limited access to system resources and can only execute non-privileged instructions. This is to prevent user-mode processes from accidentally or intentionally damaging the system or interfering with other processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Round-robin scheduling is __________.
m_Item1.Explanation: Preemptive..because process gets preempted once time quantum ends.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Using the SJF algorithm, which process is allocated to the CPU first?

Options.OptionText: The process that requests the CPU first.
The process that requests the CPU last.
The process with the smallest CPU execution time.

m_Item1.Explanation: Smallest process will get the CPU first.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which process is allocated  CPU first in FCFS algorithm?

Options.OptionText: The process that requests the CPU first
The process that requests the CPU last
Processes are allocated the CPU randomly

m_Item1.Explanation: Process that requests CPU first ...hence called as FCFS.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which state, processor executes its instructions?
m_Item1.Explanation: Running : Once the process has been assigned to a processor by the OS scheduler, the process state is set to running and the processor executes its instructions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: For which of the following purpose is Banker's algorithm used........
m_Item1.Explanation: Banker's Algorithm is mainly used to prevent deadlock.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ____________  selects the process that is ready to execute by CUP.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes a process in the context of an operating system?

Options.OptionText: A collection of programs that manage system resources.
A program in execution along with its current state.
A specific task that is waiting to be scheduled.
A system call that requests a service from the kernel.

m_Item1.Explanation: A process is a program in execution, encapsulating the program code and its current state including CPU register values, program counter, and allocated memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary function of a process control block (PCB)?

Options.OptionText: To ensure the system memory is adequately partitioned.
To store the state information of a process for management by the operating system.
To manage physical and virtual memory.
To prevent data loss during multitasking operations.

m_Item1.Explanation: The process control block (PCB) holds all the necessary information for the operating system to manage and switch between processes, including the process state, registers, scheduling information, and memory management information.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a multitasking operating system, what is the primary purpose of context switching between processes?

Options.OptionText: To terminate a process that has exceeded its time limit.
To enable efficient memory management among processes.
To allow multiple processes to share the CPU effectively.
To update all process states in the memory.

m_Item1.Explanation: Context switching is fundamental in multitasking environments, allowing the CPU to switch from running one process to another, providing the illusion of concurrency and effective CPU utilization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following components is NOT typically part of a process in an operating system?
m_Item1.Explanation: A device queue is not a part of a process; it is used by the operating system to manage waiting devices. A process includes a program counter, stack, and heap for storing code and dynamic and static data respectively.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What role does the process scheduler play in managing system processes?

Options.OptionText: It schedules which processes have priority access to I/O devices.
It determines which processes are allowed to modify kernel data.
It decides the order in which processes will be executed by the CPU.
It identifies file permissions for each process.

m_Item1.Explanation: The process scheduler is responsible for determining the order in which processes run, ensuring efficient use of the CPU by prioritizing processes based on scheduling algorithms.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a characteristic of preemptive scheduling?

Options.OptionText: Processes run to completion without interruption.
A process can be removed from the CPU to allow another process to run.
Once a process starts execution, it cannot be stopped until it completes.
Processes are scheduled in the order of completion.

m_Item1.Explanation: Preemptive scheduling allows a process to be interrupted and moved to the 'ready' state to allocate CPU time to another process. This can occur in response to external events like the arrival of higher-priority tasks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which type of scheduling are processes not interrupted until they voluntarily release the CPU?
m_Item1.Explanation: Non-Preemptive scheduling allows processes to execute until they either complete or voluntarily yield control of the CPU, essentially not being interrupted by the operating system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a main advantage of preemptive scheduling over non-preemptive scheduling?

Options.OptionText: It improves response time for interactive users.
It reduces the overhead of process scheduling.

m_Item1.Explanation: Preemptive scheduling is beneficial for interactive and real-time systems where quick response times are crucial. It allows the system to respond quickly to external events and prioritize time-critical tasks.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scheduling algorithms is non-preemptive?
m_Item1.Explanation: First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm where processes are scheduled according to their arrival order and are allowed to run to completion without interruption.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does preemptive priority scheduling decide which process to execute next?

Options.OptionText: By selecting the process with the shortest burst time.
By selecting the process that has been in the waiting queue the longest.
By selecting the process with the highest priority that is ready to run.
By selecting processes round-robin till their completion.

m_Item1.Explanation: In preemptive priority scheduling, the process with the highest priority in the ready queue is selected for execution. If a new process with higher priority arrives, it can preempt the currently running process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of process creation in operating systems, what is a process control block (PCB)?

Options.OptionText: A data structure in the operating system kernel containing the information needed to manage a particular process.
Software used to compile cross-platform applications.
A block of code executed upon process creation.
The memory segment where the process executes.

m_Item1.Explanation: The process control block (PCB) is a data structure used by the operating system to store all the information about a process, including its state, program counter, registers, memory limits, and list of open files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following system calls is used in UNIX to create a new process?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'fork()' system call is used in UNIX to create a new process. It creates a child process that is an exact duplicate of the parent process, except for the return values.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens to the Process ID (PID) of a terminated child process in Unix-like operating systems if the parent process has not called wait()?

Options.OptionText: It is immediately reclaimed by the system.
It remains in a zombie state until the parent process calls wait().
The child process gets automatically re-executed.
The PID is transferred to another process.

m_Item1.Explanation: When a child process terminates, its exit status is preserved as a 'zombie' process entry if its parent has not yet called 'wait()' to read the exit status.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which state represents a process that has terminated but its information is still available in the process table?
m_Item1.Explanation: A 'zombie' state is when a process has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table, indicating it has not been fully reaped by its parent process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a reason for process termination?
m_Item1.Explanation: Time slice expiration itself does not terminate a process; instead, it causes the process to enter a waiting state for another CPU allocation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the correct sequence of process states in a typical process life cycle?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a typical process life cycle, a process starts in the New state, moves to Ready, then Running, and finally to Terminated. It may move to Waiting/Blocked if it requires an event or resource.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following state transitions of a process signifies the allocation of CPU to the process?
m_Item1.Explanation: The transition from Ready to Running signifies that the process has been allocated the CPU and is now executing instructions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a preemptive multitasking system, a running process is moved to which state upon a time slice expiration?
m_Item1.Explanation: In preemptive multitasking, when a process's time slice expires, it is moved from Running to Ready, making room for another process to execute.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What occurs when a process moves from the Running state to the Waiting state?

Options.OptionText: The process is waiting for some I/O operation
The process is waiting to be scheduled by the CPU
The process is terminated due to an error

m_Item1.Explanation: A process moves from Running to Waiting when it requires some I/O operation to complete or waits for some resources to become available.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which mechanism does the OS use to bring a process from the Waiting state back to the Ready state?
m_Item1.Explanation: A process in the Waiting state moves to the Ready state when the awaited event or signal it was waiting for completes or is available.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a possible state of a process in Unix operating systems?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Unix systems, common process states include Running, Sleeping, and Zombie. The Suspended state is not typically a defined state in Unix-like operating systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens to a process in the Terminated state?

Options.OptionText: It remains in main memory until explicitly removed
It is removed from the process table immediately
It waits in pre-scheduler queue for another execution cycle
The operating system may still use the process's exit status code

m_Item1.Explanation: Processes in the Terminated state are not active, but the OS may use their exit status codes, especially if a parent process wants to inquire about its child's exit status using system calls like wait().

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which state does a process reside if it is ready to run but waiting for the CPU?
m_Item1.Explanation: A process is in the Ready state when it is prepared to execute but is waiting for the CPU to be allocated to it.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does a zombie process indicate in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: A process in transition from Waiting to Ready
An orphan process waiting for a re-parent
A child process correctly terminated but with an exit status not yet read
A process that is using excessive CPU resources

m_Item1.Explanation: A zombie process is a process that has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table because its parent has not read its exit status.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the PRIMARY reason for a process to move from the Waiting state back to the Ready state?

Options.OptionText: Exceeding maximum time in the Waiting state

m_Item1.Explanation: A process typically moves from Waiting to Ready upon completion of an I/O operation or when the resource it was waiting for becomes available.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a synchronization tool used to ensure that two concurrent processes do not access a critical section at the same time?
m_Item1.Explanation: Semaphores are used to solve critical section problems by ensuring that no two processes are in their critical sections at the same time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which problem is specifically addressed by using the concept of monitors in operating systems?
m_Item1.Explanation: Monitors help prevent race conditions by providing a mechanism for ensuring that only one process can execute a monitor procedure at any time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In process synchronization, what is the critical section?

Options.OptionText: A segment of code where the process spends most of its execution time
A part of a program that accesses shared resources and must not be concurrently accessed by more than one process
The portion of an operating system responsible for managing processes
A segment of code that handles all input and output operations

m_Item1.Explanation: The critical section is a segment of code where processes access shared resources that must be protected to prevent erroneous behavior.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of a mutex lock in process synchronization?

Options.OptionText: To manage memory allocation between processes
To prevent context switches from occurring during execution
To ensure that only one thread can access a critical section at a time
To initiate processes in the proper order

m_Item1.Explanation: A mutex lock ensures mutual exclusion by allowing only one thread at a time to access a critical section, thereby preventing race conditions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a characteristic of a good solution to the critical section problem?
m_Item1.Explanation: A good critical section solution requires that only one process accesses the critical section at a time, as opposed to unlimited simultaneous access.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main issue process synchronization addresses in a multi-core processor system?

Options.OptionText: Data inconsistency due to concurrent access

m_Item1.Explanation: Process synchronization in multi-core systems is mainly concerned with data inconsistency that can result from concurrent access to shared resources.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following solutions provides both mutual exclusion and solves starvation in process synchronization?
m_Item1.Explanation: The Bakery algorithm simulates a bakery queue and is designed to provide both mutual exclusion and fairness (no starvation).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduling algorithm has the potential to cause starvation without proper process synchronization?
m_Item1.Explanation: Priority scheduling can cause starvation if high-priority tasks continuously prevent low-priority tasks from being executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of a multiprocessing operating system?

Options.OptionText: It allows multiple CPUs to execute multiple processes simultaneously.
It reduces power consumption by turning off idle processors.

m_Item1.Explanation: Multiprocessing operating systems allow multiple CPUs to execute multiple processes simultaneously, improving performance and efficiency.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a challenge in designing a multiprocessing operating system?
m_Item1.Explanation: Process synchronization and resource sharing are significant challenges because multiple processors need to access shared resources without conflict.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduling algorithm is most suited for a symmetric multiprocessing system?
m_Item1.Explanation: Load balancing is crucial in symmetric multiprocessing systems to ensure that workload is evenly distributed among all processors.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a multiprocessing environment, which component ensures that processes do not interfere with each other?
m_Item1.Explanation: Process synchronization mechanisms are used to prevent race conditions and ensure processes do not improperly interfere with each other.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which type of multiprocessing system uses a master-slave architecture?
m_Item1.Explanation: Asymmetric multiprocessing uses a master-slave architecture where the master processor controls the system, while other processors execute user processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the `fork()` system call in operating systems?

Options.OptionText: To allocate more memory to the current process

m_Item1.Explanation: The `fork()` system call is used to create a new process by duplicating the current process. It creates a child process that is a copy of the parent process, except for the returned PID.

m_Item1.QuestionText: After a successful `fork()` call, what value does the child process receive?

Options.OptionText: The Process ID of the parent
The Process ID of the child

m_Item1.Explanation: After a successful `fork()` call, the child process receives a return value of 0, while the parent process receives the Process ID of the child.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes the relationship between parent and child processes after a `fork()` call?

Options.OptionText: They run entirely independently with no shared resources
They become differentiated processes sharing the same PID
They share the same memory space entirely

m_Item1.Explanation: After a `fork()` call, parent and child processes share open file descriptors, meaning they can read from and write to the same files.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true regarding the `fork()` system call error handling?

Options.OptionText: A successful `fork()` call returns negative process IDs
If `fork()` fails, it returns -1 in the child process
Failure of `fork()` results in returning -1 in the parent process

m_Item1.Explanation: If `fork()` fails, it returns -1, and only the parent process receives this error indication since the child process is not created.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a mechanism for interprocess communication in an operating system?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pipes are used for interprocess communication, allowing data transfer between processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of a message queue in interprocess communication?

Options.OptionText: To allow processes to read and write data asynchronously

m_Item1.Explanation: Message queues allow processes to communicate by sending and receiving messages asynchronously.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which interprocess communication mechanism allows processes to share variables?
m_Item1.Explanation: Shared memory allows multiple processes to access common variables by mapping them into their address spaces.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In POSIX systems, which system call is used to create a new pipe?
m_Item1.Explanation: The pipe() system call is used to create a unidirectional pipe for interprocess communication in POSIX systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the role of a semaphore in interprocess communication?

Options.OptionText: To synchronize access to shared resources

m_Item1.Explanation: Semaphores are used to synchronize access to shared resources to prevent race conditions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which interprocess communication method provides a communication link between two processes running on the same system?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pipes create a channel of communication between two processes on the same system.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a primary drawback of using shared memory for interprocess communication?
m_Item1.Explanation: Shared memory requires explicit synchronization to control access, which can be complex and error-prone.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about sockets in interprocess communication?

Options.OptionText: They work only on Windows OS
They can be used only for processes on the same machine
They enable network-based communication between processes
They do not support bidirectional communication

m_Item1.Explanation: Sockets are used to enable network-based communication, allowing processes on different machines to exchange data.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements best describes a thread in the context of an operating system?

Options.OptionText: A thread is an independent program execution flow that shares resources with other threads in the same process.
A thread is a type of process that does not share resources with other threads.
A thread is a collection of processes that share a common memory space.
A thread is an element of a process that manages input/output operations exclusively.

m_Item1.Explanation: A thread is a basic unit of CPU utilization, forming part of a process, that enables it to perform executions concurrently while sharing process resources, such as memory, with other threads.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a major advantage of using threads over multiple processes in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: Threads allow for the complete isolation of processes, reducing inter-process communication.
Threads can execute simultaneously on multiple processors, but processes cannot.
Threads incur less overhead for creation and context switching compared to processes.
Threads provide a higher degree of security than processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Threads share the same process resources, like memory space, which reduces the overhead during creation and context switching compared to processes, which require separate memory spaces.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In which threading model does the user thread directly map to one kernel thread?
m_Item1.Explanation: In the One-to-One model, each user thread corresponds directly to a kernel thread, allowing concurrent execution and better performance on multi-core systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operating system structure primarily benefits from multithreading?
m_Item1.Explanation: Real-time operating systems benefit greatly from multithreading as it allows tasks to be scheduled and executed concurrently, helping meet time-critical task deadlines.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the Many-to-Many threading model improve upon the limitations of the One-to-One model?

Options.OptionText: By reducing context switching between kernel threads.
By allowing user threads to be multiplexed over a smaller or equal number of kernel threads.
By completely avoiding the need for kernel threads.
By allocating a separate CPU core to every user thread.

m_Item1.Explanation: The Many-to-Many model allows multiple user threads to be mapped to a smaller or equal number of kernel threads, which efficiently uses system resources and reduces overhead compared to the One-to-One model.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary benefit of using multithreaded programming in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: Improves resource utilization by allowing concurrent execution

m_Item1.Explanation: Multithreaded programming allows multiple threads to be executed concurrently, which can take full advantage of the CPU resources and improve overall performance and resource utilization.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a multithreaded process, which component is shared among all threads?
m_Item1.Explanation: In a multithreaded environment, all threads share the heap memory but have their own stack, registers, and program counter.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a common issue when implementing multithreaded programs?

Options.OptionText: Memory leaks due to garbage collection

m_Item1.Explanation: Deadlocks are a common challenge in multithreaded programs, occurring when two or more threads wait indefinitely for resources held by each other.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which threading model maps each user thread to a kernel thread?
m_Item1.Explanation: The One-to-One model maps each user-level thread to a kernel-level thread, allowing multiple threads to run in parallel on multiple processors.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Java, which of the following methods is used to start the execution of a thread?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Java, the start() method is used to begin the execution of a thread, allowing the thread to move to the Runnable state.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following best describes a thread in the context of operating systems?

Options.OptionText: A thread is a standalone program that runs independently of a process.
A thread is the smallest unit of processing that can be scheduled by an operating system.
A thread is a collection of processes working together to execute a task.
A thread is a type of hardware processor that the operating system manages.

m_Item1.Explanation: A thread is the smallest unit of processing that can be scheduled by an operating system. It runs within the context of a process and shares resources associated with that process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a key difference between a thread and a process in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: Threads have separate memory spaces, while processes share the same memory.
Processes cannot run concurrently, but threads can.
Threads within the same process share memory space, while processes do not.
Processes are always faster to create than threads.

m_Item1.Explanation: Threads within the same process share the process’s memory space, while different processes have separate memory spaces. This allows efficient communication between threads but requires mechanisms for inter-process communication (IPC) for processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of operating systems, which statement about process control blocks (PCBs) and thread control blocks (TCBs) is correct?

Options.OptionText: Both PCBs and TCBs store registers, scheduling information, and state information.
PCBs do not exist in modern operating systems, only TCBs do.
PCBs are used to manage only threads, while TCBs manage processes.
Only PCBs need to keep track of memory mapping information in multi-threaded applications.

m_Item1.Explanation: Both PCBs and TCBs store necessary information for process and thread execution, including registers, scheduling, and state information. However, PCBs have more information related to process execution such as memory mappings.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the overhead of creating a thread compare to that of creating a process?

Options.OptionText: Creating a thread generally has the same overhead as creating a process.
Creating a thread generally has more overhead than creating a process.
Creating a thread generally has less overhead than creating a process.
The overhead of creating a thread and a process is negligible, hence almost the same.

m_Item1.Explanation: Creating a thread generally has less overhead than creating a process because threads within the same process share resources such as memory, while creating a new process requires setting up a separate memory space and resources.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following aspects is exclusive to processes but not to individual threads?

Options.OptionText: Making system calls to the operating system.
Sharing code and heap memory with other execution contexts.

m_Item1.Explanation: Processes have independent resource allocation, which includes memory space, process tables, and file descriptors. Threads, on the other hand, share much of the process-level resources such as code and heap memory with other threads within the same process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the waitpid system call in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: To send a signal to a process to terminate it.
To wait for a specific child process to change state.
To create a new process by duplicating the calling process.
To allocate memory for user processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: The waitpid system call is used to wait for a specific child process to change state, such as ending or being stopped. It provides more control over which child process is being waited on compared to the wait system call.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which parameter of the waitpid system call specifies whether the calling process should wait for any child process or a specific child process?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'pid' parameter in the waitpid function specifies which child process the parent process should wait for. It can be set to -1 to wait for any child process, or a positive number to wait for a specific process ID.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If the options parameter of waitpid is set to WNOHANG, what will the system call do?

Options.OptionText: It will hang until the process exits.
It will return immediately if no child process has exited.
It will terminate the process immediately.
It will wait indefinitely regardless of any child process states.

m_Item1.Explanation: If the WNOHANG option is specified, the waitpid system call will return immediately if no child has exited. This allows the calling process to continue execution without blocking.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is returned by the waitpid system call when no specified child process is available to be waited upon?

Options.OptionText: The process ID of the child
The status of the last child process

m_Item1.Explanation: The waitpid function returns -1 if there are no child processes that match the specified criteria, and the errno variable is set to indicate the error (e.g., ECHILD if there are no child processes).

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an appropriate scenario for using waitpid with the WUNTRACED option?

Options.OptionText: To wait for a child process to finish execution.
To ignore the termination of zombie processes.
To wait for stopped children that have not been reported since stopped.
To simultaneously terminate all running child processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: The WUNTRACED option is used with waitpid to make the call return for children that are stopped and have not been reported since they stopped. This is useful in job control to determine if child processes have been paused.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary purpose of the 'exec' system call in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: To replace the current process image with a new one
To allocate additional memory to a process

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'exec' system call replaces the current process image with a new program. It is used to run a different program within the same process ID, without creating a new process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens to the process ID of a process that executes an 'exec' system call?

Options.OptionText: It is changed to a new unique process ID
It remains the same as the prior process

m_Item1.Explanation: During an 'exec' system call, the process ID remains the same as the process image is replaced, but the execution context stays under the same process ID.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following 'exec' family functions does NOT provide an option to specify environment variables separately?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'execlp' function does not take an explicit environment variable argument; it uses the existing environment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If a process successfully completes an 'exec' call, what happens to the local variables of the original process?

Options.OptionText: They are retained and accessible by the new program
They are lost and replaced by the new process's variables
They are converted to global variables
They are saved to a temporary file

m_Item1.Explanation: Once 'exec' completes successfully, the original process's memory—including local variables—is replaced, so they are lost and replaced by those of the new program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which system call would typically be used before an 'exec' call to create a new process in the UNIX operating system?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'fork' is used to create a new process by duplicating the calling process. This is typically followed by an 'exec' call in the child process to replace the process image with a new program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which system call is used in Unix-like operating systems to create a new child process?
m_Item1.Explanation: The fork() system call is used to create a new child process in Unix-like operating systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What distinguishes the child process from the parent process immediately after a fork() call?
m_Item1.Explanation: After a fork() call, the only difference between the parent and child process is the return value of fork(). The parent receives the PID of the child, while the child receives 0.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the result of a failed fork() operation in Unix-like systems?

Options.OptionText: The child process runs in place of the parent

m_Item1.Explanation: If fork() fails, it returns a value of -1 and sets the errno to indicate what error occurred.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a typical fork-exec model, what happens to the child process once exec() is called?

Options.OptionText: The child process continues running the same program as the parent
The child process is replaced by a new program
The child creates its own child process

m_Item1.Explanation: The exec() system call replaces the child's memory space with a new program, effectively transforming the child into a new process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a zombie process in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: A process that has completed execution but still has an entry in the process table.
A process that is waiting for an event to occur.
A child process that has lost its parent process.
A process that runs without consuming CPU resources.

m_Item1.Explanation: A zombie process is a process that has finished executing but still has an entry in the process table to allow the parent process to read its exit status.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following situations leads to an orphan process?

Options.OptionText: A child process is terminated before the parent process.
A parent process is terminated while its child processes are still running.
Both parent and child processes are terminated simultaneously.
The system performs a forceful restart.

m_Item1.Explanation: An orphan process occurs when a parent process terminates while its child processes are still running, leaving them without a parent.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does an operating system typically handle a zombie process?

Options.OptionText: By forcefully terminating it using the kill command.
By cleaning up its entry once the parent process executes a wait system call.
By reassigning it to another parent process.
By allowing it to eventually consume system resources until reboot.

m_Item1.Explanation: A zombie process is removed from the process table when the parent process calls wait(), allowing the system to free resources.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens to an orphan process in a Unix-like operating system?

Options.OptionText: It gets terminated immediately by the operating system.
It is adopted by the init process (PID 1).
It continues running but with limited privileges.

m_Item1.Explanation: In Unix-like systems, the init process adopts orphan processes, allowing them to continue running and ensuring proper management.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is the primary function of a long-term scheduler in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: Swap processes in and out of memory

m_Item1.Explanation: The long-term scheduler, also known as the job scheduler, is responsible for controlling the degree of multiprogramming by selecting processes from the pool and loading them into memory for execution.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduler is responsible for allocating the CPU to processes in a time-sharing environment?
m_Item1.Explanation: The short-term scheduler, also known as the CPU scheduler, is responsible for selecting the process that will get the CPU next in a time-sharing environment.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary role of a medium-term scheduler in modern operating systems?

Options.OptionText: Swapping processes in and out of the main memory

m_Item1.Explanation: The medium-term scheduler swaps processes in and out of the main memory to manage the multiprogramming environment effectively, providing a balance between CPU utilization and context switching.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What characteristic typically differentiates short-term scheduling from the other types of scheduling?

Options.OptionText: Execution and dispatching every few milliseconds

m_Item1.Explanation: Short-term scheduling involves the execution of processes and dispatches CPU scheduling decisions every few milliseconds, making it a frequent and time-critical operation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduler determines which jobs are to be admitted to the system for processing?
m_Item1.Explanation: The long-term scheduler controls the admission of new processes into the system. It decides which jobs or processes should be admitted to the ready queue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary responsibility of the short-term scheduler in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: Manage swapping of processes in and out of memory
Select which process should be executed next by the CPU

m_Item1.Explanation: The short-term scheduler, also known as the CPU scheduler, selects one of the processes in the ready queue to be executed by the CPU next.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduler is primarily concerned with improving the process mix in the memory, enhancing multitasking?
m_Item1.Explanation: The medium-term scheduler is responsible for swapping processes in and out of the memory to improve process mix and system responsiveness, thus enhancing multitasking.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following correctly describes a function of the long-term scheduler in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: It manages the operating system's memory allocation.
It controls the degree of multiprogramming in the system.
It decides when a new process should be allocated CPU time.
It handles the context switching among processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: The long-term scheduler determines the admission of processes into the system, thereby controlling the degree of multiprogramming, which is the number of processes in memory.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following describes a scenario where a medium-term scheduler intervenes?

Options.OptionText: When a process is being created and needs to be admitted into the system.
When a process needs to be temporarily removed from memory to improve response time.
When the CPU is idle and needs a process to execute next.
When the operating system needs to handle process synchronization.

m_Item1.Explanation: The medium-term scheduler intervenes by swapping processes in and out of the memory temporarily, typically to improve system performance and response time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following process scheduling algorithms may result in starvation?
m_Item1.Explanation: Shortest Job Next (SJN) may result in starvation if shorter processes continuously arrive while longer processes wait indefinitely.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In Round Robin scheduling, what does the time quantum represent?

Options.OptionText: The total time a process runs before being terminated
The fixed time a process runs before moving to the next process
The total CPU burst time of a process
The maximum wait time in the queue

m_Item1.Explanation: The time quantum in Round Robin scheduling is the fixed time that a process runs before the CPU switches to the next process in the queue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about the priority scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It executes processes in a randomly determined order.
It executes processes based on their order of arrival.
It executes the highest-priority processes first.
It executes the processes having the lowest burst time first.

m_Item1.Explanation: Priority scheduling selects and executes processes based on their priority values, giving precedence to higher-priority processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which process scheduling algorithm can be characterized as preemptive and priority-based?
m_Item1.Explanation: Priority Scheduling with Preemption allows a higher-priority process to preempt a running lower-priority process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a multi-level queue scheduling algorithm, what is the main goal of separate queues?

Options.OptionText: To minimize the total waiting time
To separate processes based on their memory requirements
To classify processes based on their priority or type

m_Item1.Explanation: Multi-level queue scheduling separates processes into different queues based on priority or type, allowing tailored scheduling for different categories.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main disadvantage of the First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling?

Options.OptionText: It is unfair to small processes.
It can lead to the convoy effect.
It is inefficient with I/O-bound processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: FCFS can lead to the convoy effect, where shorter or interactive processes get stuck behind longer CPU-bound processes, causing delays.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduling algorithm is considered optimal for minimizing average waiting time if the CPU burst time of all processes is known?
m_Item1.Explanation: Shortest Job First (SJF) is optimal for minimizing average waiting time when the CPU burst time for all processes is known in advance.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of CPU scheduling, what is aging?

Options.OptionText: Gradually increasing the priority of processes that wait for too long
Preemptively ending older processes that consume too much memory
Reducing CPU time for processes running for an extended period
Consolidating older processes in a separate queue

m_Item1.Explanation: Aging is a technique used to prevent starvation by gradually increasing the priority of processes that have been waiting for a long time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is correct regarding Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling?

Options.OptionText: It uses a single queue for all processes.
It moves processes between queues based on their behavior and needs.
It assigns static priorities to processes.
It guarantees no starvation for low-priority processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Multilevel Feedback Queue Scheduling allows processes to move between different priority queues based on their execution behavior, adapting to different needs dynamically.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which characteristic makes Round Robin scheduling suitable for time-sharing systems?

Options.OptionText: Use of time quantum for fair CPU time distribution
Preemptive nature to avoid CPU-bound processes

m_Item1.Explanation: Round Robin scheduling, with its time quantum, ensures fair distribution of CPU time among processes, making it suitable for time-sharing systems.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary characteristic of the First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It prioritizes processes based on their CPU burst time.
It schedules processes in the order they arrive.
It preempts running processes based on priority levels.
It uses a time quantum to allocate CPU time to processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: FCFS scheduling allocates CPU time to processes in the order they arrive in the ready queue, without considering process priority or burst time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is a disadvantage of the FCFS scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: Possible convoy effect causing longer wait times.
Increased complexity in managing the ready queue.

m_Item1.Explanation: The convoy effect can occur in FCFS scheduling if a long process blocks the queue, causing following processes to experience increased wait times.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the FCFS scheduling algorithm handle process priorities?

Options.OptionText: FCFS does not consider priorities; it runs in arrival order.
It always gives priority to processes with shorter burst times.
It processes higher priority queues first.

m_Item1.Explanation: In FCFS scheduling, all processes are treated equally without prioritizing any, simply running in the order of their arrival.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When might FCFS be more suitable compared to other scheduling algorithms?

Options.OptionText: When the system has processes that vary widely in priority.
When there's a need for minimal scheduling overhead.
When optimal response time is necessary for applications.
When process context switching needs to be frequent.

m_Item1.Explanation: FCFS is simpler and has minimal scheduling overhead, making it suitable when system resources are limited or simplicity is more critical.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What type of system best benefits from the FCFS scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: Time-sharing systems requiring fair process execution.
Real-time systems where deadlines are critical.
Batch processing systems with predictable workloads.
Interactive systems needing fast response times.

m_Item1.Explanation: Batch processing systems benefit from FCFS as it suits their predictable and non-interactive workload processing, and responsiveness is not a critical factor.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements best describes the Shortest Job First (SJF) algorithm?

Options.OptionText: SJF is a preemptive scheduling algorithm that always chooses the process that arrived first.
SJF is a scheduling algorithm that executes the smallest process that hasn't been completed.
SJF is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm that selects the job with the shortest burst time to execute next.
SJF is a scheduling algorithm that allocates CPU based on priority.

m_Item1.Explanation: Shortest Job First (SJF) is a non-preemptive scheduling algorithm that selects the process with the smallest burst time to execute next. It's designed to minimize the average waiting time for a set of jobs.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a system using the Shortest Job First algorithm, which criterion is primarily used to determine the next process to execute?
m_Item1.Explanation: Shortest Job First (SJF) uses the burst time of processes as the primary criterion to decide which process should execute next. The process with the shortest burst time is selected to minimize waiting time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is one major disadvantage of using the Shortest Job First scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It leads to the starvation of longer processes.
It does not consider the arrival time of processes.
It requires no information about the burst time of processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: A major drawback of the Shortest Job First algorithm is that it can lead to starvation of longer processes. If there is a continuous flow of processes with shorter burst times, longer processes may experience indefinite delays.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which variant of the Shortest Job First algorithm allows for process interruption?
m_Item1.Explanation: Preemptive SJF, also known as Shortest Remaining Time First (SRTF), allows process interruption. A new process with a shorter remaining burst time can preempt the currently executing process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Why is the Shortest Job First algorithm considered optimal in terms of average waiting time?

Options.OptionText: It always executes processes in the order they arrive.
It minimizes the number of context switches.
It ensures that the next smallest process is executed, which reduces the total time other processes have to wait.
It schedules processes based on their priority levels.

m_Item1.Explanation: SJF is optimal in terms of average waiting time because it executes processes with the shortest burst time first, reducing the total waiting time for subsequent processes. This approach minimizes overall average waiting time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary characteristic of a priority scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: Processes are executed in the order they arrive
Each process is assigned a priority level
Processes are executed based on their memory requirements
All processes share the CPU equally

m_Item1.Explanation: In a priority scheduling algorithm, each process is assigned a specific priority level, and the CPU is allocated to the process with the highest priority.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following could be a disadvantage of using a priority scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: Uniform response time for all processes

m_Item1.Explanation: A disadvantage of priority scheduling is that lower priority processes may suffer from starvation if higher priority processes continuously arrive and are executed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does priority inversion occur in a priority scheduling system?

Options.OptionText: When a higher priority process waits for a lower priority process to release a resource
When lower priority processes execute before higher priority ones
When all processes have the same priority
When CPU allocation cannot be changed dynamically

m_Item1.Explanation: Priority inversion occurs when a higher priority process is forced to wait because a lower priority process holds a needed resource.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which mechanism can be used to prevent starvation in a priority scheduling algorithm?
m_Item1.Explanation: Aging is a technique used to gradually increase the priority of processes that wait in the system for a long time, thus preventing starvation.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In preemptive priority scheduling, what can cause a process to lose the CPU?

Options.OptionText: A lower priority process receives data from input
A higher priority process enters the ready queue
The base priority of the process is changed to zero

m_Item1.Explanation: In preemptive priority scheduling, if a higher priority process becomes ready, it can preempt the CPU from a currently running lower priority process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of using the Round Robin (RR) algorithm for process scheduling over First-Come, First-Served (FCFS)?

Options.OptionText: It minimizes the total turnaround time.
It provides equal CPU time to all processes, preventing any single process from dominating the CPU.
It always results in the shortest completion time.

m_Item1.Explanation: Round Robin scheduling allocates a fixed time quantum to each process, ensuring that all processes are treated fairly and prevent any single process from monopolizing the CPU.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In the context of the Round Robin algorithm, what does the term 'time quantum' refer to?

Options.OptionText: The maximum time a process can spend in the waiting queue.
The maximum number of processes that can be handled at once by the CPU.
The fixed time period during which a process is allowed to run on the CPU before being pre-empted.
The total time required to complete all tasks in the system.

m_Item1.Explanation: The time quantum is a small, fixed time period that determines how long a process can run before it is switched out to allow another process to execute. This ensures fairness in CPU scheduling.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following disadvantages is associated with a very small time quantum in a Round Robin scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: Decreased response time for all processes.
High context switch overhead due to too frequent process switching.

m_Item1.Explanation: A very small time quantum will cause the CPU to switch tasks too frequently, leading to high context switch overhead as more time is spent switching between processes than executing them.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does Round Robin scheduling differ when there are processes with the same burst time and equal number of processes?

Options.OptionText: It results in the longest average waiting time.
It processes the last queued process first.
It makes no difference; all processes still receive equal time quanta ensuring fair scheduling.
It gives priority to the processes with the shortest burst time.

m_Item1.Explanation: In Round Robin scheduling, each process gets an equal time quantum, ensuring fair scheduling. Therefore, if all processes have the same burst time, they still receive equal execution time regardless of their position in the queue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens when the time quantum is set too large in a Round Robin scheduling environment?

Options.OptionText: The RR algorithm degenerates to a First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling.
It reduces system throughput due to infrequent context switches.
Increased process starvation occurs due to imbalanced CPU sharing.
All processes are executed in parallel without context switches.

m_Item1.Explanation: If the time quantum is too large, Round Robin behaves like FCFS because each process will run to completion in its allotted time without being preempted, leading to long wait times for other processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the primary advantage of using a Round Robin scheduling algorithm for process queues in an operating system?

Options.OptionText: It guarantees that all processes are handled without starvation.
It prioritizes processes based on their priorities.
It reduces the average waiting time for high priority processes.

m_Item1.Explanation: Round Robin scheduling treats all processes equally by assigning them a fixed time slice, ensuring that no process is starved of CPU time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scheduling algorithms can lead to convoy effect?
m_Item1.Explanation: The First-Come, First-Served scheduling algorithm can lead to the convoy effect, where short processes get stuck waiting behind long processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the priority scheduling algorithm differ from the Round Robin scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: Priority scheduling uses a fixed time quantum.
Round Robin prioritizes shorter jobs over longer ones.
Round Robin minimizes average turnaround time.

m_Item1.Explanation: Priority scheduling assigns CPU based on priority, which can lead to starvation of low-priority processes, unlike Round Robin which gives equal CPU time to all processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement best describes the First-Come, First-Served (FCFS) scheduling algorithm?

Options.OptionText: It is the most efficient scheduling algorithm for all scenarios.
Processes are executed in the order of their arrival.
It always results in the lowest average waiting time.
FCFS uses a priority-based queue for process selection.

m_Item1.Explanation: The FCFS scheduling algorithm operates by executing processes in the exact order they arrive in the queue, without considering priority or execution time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is Belady's Anomaly in the context of process scheduling?

Options.OptionText: An increase in the number of page frames leading to more page faults.
A decrease in the number of CPUs leading to increased scheduling complexity.
A scenario where faster CPU performance decreases system efficiency.
A situation where more processes result in better CPU utilization.

m_Item1.Explanation: Belady's Anomaly refers to a counterintuitive situation in certain page replacement algorithms, where increasing the number of page frames results in an increased number of page faults.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which page replacement algorithm is most commonly associated with Belady's Anomaly?
m_Item1.Explanation: Belady's Anomaly is most commonly associated with the FIFO page replacement algorithm, where increasing the number of page frames can sometimes lead to an increase in page faults.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When does Belady's Anomaly occur in the FIFO page replacement algorithm?

Options.OptionText: When the program's working set size exceeds the number of page frames.
When the program is too large to fit in memory.
When the number of page frames is increased but results in more page faults.
When the pages are replaced in the order they arrive.

m_Item1.Explanation: Belady's Anomaly occurs in the FIFO page replacement algorithm when an increase in the number of page frames leads to an increase in page faults due to the non-optimal replacement of pages.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How can Belady's Anomaly be mitigated in page replacement strategies?

Options.OptionText: By using a random page replacement algorithm.
By employing a page replacement algorithm like LRU that does not exhibit Belady's Anomaly.
By decreasing the number of page frames available.
By optimizing the CPU scheduling algorithm.

m_Item1.Explanation: Employing a page replacement algorithm like Least Recently Used (LRU), which does not exhibit Belady's Anomaly, can help mitigate this anomaly.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a multilevel scheduling algorithm, which of the following is a primary reason for using multiple queues?

Options.OptionText: To segregate processes based on characteristics such as CPU-burst time
To limit the number of processes in the system

m_Item1.Explanation: Multilevel scheduling utilizes multiple queues to categorize processes based on specific characteristics, such as CPU-burst time, allowing processes with similar attributes to be managed more efficiently.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which scheduling method is most likely to be used in a lower-priority queue within a multilevel feedback queue?
m_Item1.Explanation: In lower-priority queues of a multilevel feedback queue, a Round Robin (RR) scheduling with a large quantum is typically used to allow longer execution times for processes that are demoted due to higher execution history or lower priority.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of multilevel queue scheduling?

Options.OptionText: Each queue has its own scheduling algorithm
Processes are allowed to move between queues
Strict separation of different types of processes

m_Item1.Explanation: Multilevel queue scheduling strictly differentiates between queues, and processes typically do not move between queues unless it is a multilevel feedback queue.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a key advantage of using multilevel scheduling algorithms with feedback?

Options.OptionText: Better handling of I/O-bound and CPU-bound processes

m_Item1.Explanation: Multilevel scheduling algorithms with feedback adjust the priority of processes over time based on their behavior, allowing better handling and differentiation between I/O-bound and CPU-bound processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In a three-level scheduling algorithm, what is the primary function of the middle queue?

Options.OptionText: To serve as an intermediate for processes transitioning between foreground and background

m_Item1.Explanation: The middle queue in a three-level scheduling algorithm typically serves as an intermediate stage for processes transitioning between the foreground (interactive) and background (batch) queues, often using different scheduling disciplines than the two extremes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: System calls are invoked by using __________.
m_Item1.Explanation:
System calls are invoked by using a software interrupt, which is a special type of interrupt that is generated by the CPU in response to a request from a user-mode process.  
Polling is a technique in which a process repeatedly checks the status of a device or resource to determine if it is ready for use.
Indirect jump is a type of jump instruction in which the target address is specified indirectly, through a register or memory location.
A privileged instruction is an instruction that can only be executed in system mode and is typically used to perform low-level system tasks

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which method is used by a program to make request to operating system?
m_Item1.Explanation: request to operating system is generated by a system call. When any program wants to communicate with OS a call is given to Kernel and it is call ed as a system call.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Page fault occurs when ___________.

Options.OptionText: The page is corrupted by application software
The page is in main memory
The page is not in main memory
One tries to divide a number by 0

m_Item1.Explanation:
A page fault occurs when a running program requests a page that is not currently loaded in main memory. In virtual memory systems, the operating system divides the physical memory into fixed-size blocks called pages. When a program needs to access a particular page, the page is loaded into main memory from the disk.
However, if the page is not currently in main memory, the processor generates a page fault interrupt. The operating system then loads the required page into main memory from the disk and restarts the instruction that caused the page fault.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a necessary condition for a deadlock?
m_Item1.Explanation: All of the options listed (Mutual Exclusion, Circular wait, and No preemption of resources) are necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The computer itself uses __________ language.
m_Item1.Explanation: Computer uses machine level language, i.e 0 and 1. This is the only language which computer understands.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which CPU scheduling algorithm is non- preemptive type from the following?

Options.OptionText: First come first serve based scheduling

m_Item1.Explanation: FCFS is non preemptive algorithm since this algorithm executes any process till the end whatever the size of process may. Process taken for execution will never be kept aside hence it is called as non-preemptive.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true from the following?

Options.OptionText: A safe state is a deadlock state always
An unsafe state is a deadlock state always
An unsafe state has a probability to be a deadlock state

m_Item1.Explanation: A safe state is a state of a system where there exists a safe sequence of all processes, such that if each process requests its maximum resources, then there will be no deadlock. In other words, a safe state guarantees that the system can avoid deadlock.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true for the deadlock?

Options.OptionText: When a process terminates, it becomes dead process and this leads to dead lock
Deadlock arises when a process tries  to access a non shareable resource
Deadlock arises when process is holding some more resources that are already taken by some other process and no one wants to release their resources
Deadlock arises when we try to lock the process and the process is in running state that lock become a dead lock

m_Item1.Explanation: Deadlock is a situation in which two or more processes are blocked because each is holding a resource that the other needs. In other words, a deadlock occurs when a set of processes are waiting for each other to release resources, resulting in a circular wait.

m_Item1.QuestionText: By using interrupt which kind of problem will be eliminated?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Interrupts are used to eliminate the need for polling in I/O operations. Polling is a technique in which the CPU continuously checks the status of an I/O device to determine whether it is ready to receive or send data. This can be inefficient because the CPU may spend a lot of time waiting for the I/O device to become ready.
Therefore, the statement "By using interrupt, polling problem will be eliminated" is true. Spooling, job scheduling, and none of the above are not related to interrupts.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Binary Semaphores are used for _______________
m_Item1.Explanation: Binary semaphores can be used to implement mutual exclusion, which is a technique for ensuring that only one process or thread can access a shared resource at a time. In this case, the binary semaphore is used to control access to a critical section of code, where the shared resource is accessed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which CPU scheduling algorithm is non-preemptive type from the following?

Options.OptionText: First come first serve based scheduling

m_Item1.Explanation: FCFS is non premptive type of algorithm . FCFS will execute any process till the end whatever size of process comes for execution. It will not preempt any process.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will be the possibility, for a process to go in a wait or block state?

Options.OptionText: due to the higher priority process arrival

m_Item1.Explanation: Process will go in a block/wait state if a process need to access a disk or it's time quatam expires or any process with higher priority arrives.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What dispatcher does?

Options.OptionText: Select the process from the ready queue

An optimal scheduling algorithm in terms of minimizing the average waiting time of a given set of processes is ________.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Using Priority Scheduling algorithm, find the average waiting time for the following set of processes given with their priorities in the order:
Process : Burst Time : Priority respectively .
P1 : 10 : 3 ,
P2 : 1 : 1 ,
P3 : 2 : 4 ,
P4 : 1 : 5 ,
P5 : 5 : 2.
m_Item1.Explanation:
The lower the priority number, the higher the priority.
So, the order of the processes will be: P2, P5, P1, P3, P4
The waiting time for each process is the sum of the burst times of the processes that come before it:
P2 waits for 0 units of time (no processes come before it)
P5 waits for 1 unit of time (P2 comes before it)
P1 waits for 6 units of time (P2 and P5 come before it)
P3 waits for 16 units of time (P2, P5, and P1 come before it)
P4 waits for 18 units of time (P2, P5, P1, and P3 come before it)
The average waiting time is the sum of the waiting times divided by the number of processes:
(0 + 1 + 6 + 16 + 18) / 5 = 41 / 5 = 8.2

m_Item1.Explanation: An interrupt is a signal sent to the processor that interrupts the current execution of instructions and diverts its execution to a specific routine called the interrupt service routine (ISR). m_Item1.QuestionText: A computer system has 6 tape drives, with ‘n’ processes competing for them. Each process may need 3 tape drives. The maximum value of ‘n’ for which the system is guaranteed to be deadlock free is :
m_Item1.Explanation:
A deadlock-free system is one where no deadlock can ever occur. In this scenario, there are 6 tape drives, and each process may need 3 tape drives. To ensure that the system is deadlock-free, the maximum number of processes that can be allowed to compete for the tape drives is 2.
With 2 processes, each process can be allocated 3 tape drives without causing a deadlock, as there are 6 tape drives available in total. If a third process were allowed to compete for the tape drives, it could potentially cause a deadlock if all three processes requested all of their required tape drives simultaneously. Therefore, the maximum value of 'n' for which the system is guaranteed to be deadlock-free is 2.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A system has 3 processes sharing 4 resources. If each process needs a maximum of 2 units then, deadlock :
m_Item1.Explanation: In this scenario, there are 3 processes and 4 resources. If each process needs a maximum of 2 units, it is possible for a deadlock to occur if all processes acquire one resource unit each and then wait for another resource unit that is held by another process. However, it's not guaranteed to happen, as it depends on the order in which processes request and release resources. Therefore, the answer is that a deadlock may occur.

m_Item1.QuestionText: ‘m’ processes share ‘n’ resources of the same type. The maximum need of each process doesn’t exceed ‘n’ and the sum of all their maximum needs is always less than m+n. In this setup, deadlock :
m_Item1.Explanation:
In this scenario, there are 'm' processes and 'n' resources of the same type. The maximum need of each process doesn't exceed 'n', and the sum of all their maximum needs is always less than m+n. This means that there are always enough resources to satisfy the maximum needs of all processes, even if all processes request their maximum resources simultaneously.
Since there are always enough resources to satisfy the maximum needs of all processes, it is impossible for a deadlock to occur. Therefore, the answer is that a deadlock can never occur in this setup.

m_Item1.Explanation: This means to undo the changes made by the process since it acquired the resource. This is often necessary when a resource is preempted, so that the process can be restarted from a consistent state.

m_Item1.QuestionText: To _______ to a safe state, the system needs to keep more information about the states of processes.
m_Item1.Explanation: To roll back a process to a safe state, the system needs to keep more information about the states of processes. Rolling back a process involves undoing all the changes made by the process since its last safe state, so that the system can return to a consistent state. This requires the system to maintain a record of all the changes made by the process, as well as information about the process's current state and the resources it is holding. Aborting a process simply terminates it without undoing any changes, while queuing a process refers to placing it in a waiting state until it can be executed. Neither of these options requires the system to keep additional information about the states of processes. Therefore, the answer is that to roll back a process to a safe state, the system needs to keep more information about the states of processes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: If the resources are always pre-empted from the same process, __________ can occur.
m_Item1.Explanation: If resources are always preempted from the same process, starvation can occur. Starvation is a situation where a process is unable to obtain the resources it needs to make progress, even though those resources are available, because other processes are always given higher priority. In this scenario, if a particular process is always chosen to have its resources preempted, it may never be able to complete its task because it is constantly being deprived of the resources it needs. Over time, this can lead to starvation
